elements.sent-2411-001: Let $G$ be a connected graph and let $F$ be a connected subgraph of $G$ with a given structure.
elements.sent-2411-002: We consider that the centrality of a vertex $i$ of $G$ is determined by the centrality of other vertices in all subgraphs contain $i$ and isomorphic to $F$.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper we propose an $F$-subgraph tensor and an $F$-subgraph eigenvector centrality of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-004: When the graph is $F$-connected, we show that the $F$-subgraph tensor is weakly irreducible, and in this case, the $F$-subgraph eigenvector centrality exists.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, when we choose $F$ to be a path $P_1$ of length $1$(or a complete graph $K_2$), the $F$-eigenvector centrality is eigenvector centrality of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we propose the $(K_2,F)$-subgraph eigenvector centrality of $G$ and prove it always exists when $G$ is connected.
elements.sent-2411-007: Specifically, the $P_2$-subgraph eigenvector centrality and $(K_2,F)$-subgraph eigenvector centrality are studied.
elements.sent-2411-008: Some examples show that the ranking of vertices under them differs from the rankings under several classic centralities.
elements.sent-2411-009: Vertices of a regular graph have the same eigenvector centrality scores.
elements.sent-2411-010: But the $(K_2,K_3)$-subgraph eigenvector centrality can distinguish vertices in a given regular graph.
elements.sent-2411-011: Centrality is a measure of the importance of vertices in complex networks according to a certain viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-012: Over the years, researchers have proposed many centrality measures \cite{albert2000error,freeman1991centrality,alahakoon2011k,estrada2005subgraph,zhou2023estrada,bugedo2024family .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Eigenvector centrality is an important centrality measure \cite{bonacich1972factoring .}
elements.sent-2411-014: It is widely applied in social networks \cite{bihari2015eigenvector , brain science networks \cite{lohmann2010eigenvector , protein networks \cite{negre2018eigenvector , hypernetworks \cite{benson2019three,tudisco2018node , multilayer networks \cite{wang2018new , Google's PageRank search engine \cite{langville2005survey , and so on.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Let $A = (a_{ij )_{n \times n}$ be the adjacency matrix of graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, where \begin{equation}a_{ij}=  1, if  $ i \sim j $, 0,  otherwise. \end{equation} \begin{defi \cite{bonacich1972factoring For a connected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, let $A$ be the adjacency matrix of $G$, and $\rho(A)$ be the spectral radius of $G$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The positive solution $\boldsymbol {x =(x_1,x_2,{\cdots ,x_n)^{\mathrm{T }$ to the equation $A \boldsymbol {x = \rho(A) \boldsymbol {x}$ is the eigenvector centrality of $G$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: For vertex $i \in [n]$, from Definition \ref{ec , we know that \begin{equation}\label{90999} x_i = \frac{1}{ \rho(A)} \sum_{j=1}^n a_{ij} x_j = \frac{1}{ \rho(A)} \sum_{ \{i,j\} \in {E(G)}} x_j.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-018: That is the centrality of $i$ is determined by the centrality of other vertices that are in edges contain $i$.
elements.sent-2411-019: An edge of $G$ can be regarded as a subgraph of $G$, that is a path $P_1$ of length $1$(or a complete graph $K_2$ ).
elements.sent-2411-020: So the eigenvector centrality of $i$ is determined by the eigenvector centrality of other vertices in subgraphs $P_1$(or $K_2$) that are contain $i$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Inspired by the eigenvector centrality of graphs, we propose an $F$-subgraph eigenvector centrality of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-022: That is the centrality of a vertex $i$ of $G$ to be determined by the centrality of other vertices in all subgraphs contain $i$ and isomorphic to $F$.
elements.sent-2411-023: We propose an $F$-subgraph tensor of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-024: We use $V_F$ to denote a set consisting of vertex sets of subgraphs isomorphic to $F$.

elements.sent-2411-033: The $2$-star subgraph tensor and it's eigenvalue problems have already been proposed in \cite{petersdorf1969spektrum,cvetkovic1980spectra .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The literature \cite{liu2023high and \cite{liu2023generalization respectively proposed the $r$-star tensor and the $r$-clique tensor of graphs and used their spectra to study the extremal problems of graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The literature \cite{xu2023two proposed the 2-step tensor and the 2-step eigenvector centrality of graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In this paper, the general subgraph tensor and the general subgraph eigenvector centrality of graphs are proposed (that is the Definition \ref{ztzldy , \ref{zitudingyi ).}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Thus the definitions of subgraph tensor in \cite{cvetkovic1980spectra,liu2023high,liu2023generalization,xu2023two are special cases of Definition \ref{ztzldy .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: In this paper we consider that the centrality of a vertex $i$ of $G$ is determined by the centrality of other vertices in all subgraphs contain $i$ and isomorphic to $F$.
elements.sent-2411-039: We propose an $F$-subgraph tensor and an $F$-subgraph eigenvector centrality of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-040: Furthermore, we propose the $(K_2,F)$-eigenvector centrality of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-041: We give a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of $F$-subgraph eigenvector centrality and prove the $(K_2,F)$-subgraph eigenvector centrality always exists.
elements.sent-2411-042: The organization of this paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section 2, we introduce some concepts and lemmas related to tensors.
elements.sent-2411-044: In Section 3, we study the $P_2$-subgraph eigenvector centrality and the $(K_2,F)$-subgraph eigenvector centrality, and give the existence of the two centrality measures.
elements.sent-2411-045: The degree centrality and eigenvector centrality of the vertices are always the same in regular graphs.
elements.sent-2411-046: We provide an example showcasing that the ($K_2,K_3$)-subgraph eigenvector centrality can distinguish vertices in a regular graph.
elements.sent-2411-047: In Section 4, the centrality measures proposed in this paper are applied to some real-world networks, and the results show that the vertices with high centrality scores are generally in more given subgraphs.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, the centrality score of a vertex is not entirely determined by the number of given subgraphs contain the vertex, the centrality measures proposed in this paper are global centrality measures.
elements.sent-2411-049: Let $\mathbb{C ^n$ and $\mathbb{C ^{[k,n]}$ denote the sets of $n$-dimensional vectors and $k$-th order $n$-dimensional tensors over the complex number field $\mathbb{C}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: A tensor $\mathcal{A =(a_{{i_1 {i_2 {\cdots {i_k ) \in \mathbb{C ^{[k,n] $ is a multi-dimensional array containing $n^k$ elements, where $i_j \in [n], j \in [k]$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: For $\boldsymbol {x =(x_1,x_2,{\cdots ,x_n)^{\mathrm{T \in \mathbb{C ^n$, $\mathcal{A {\boldsymbol {x ^{k-1}$ is a vector in $\mathbb{C ^n$, with the $i$-th component \begin{equation}(A{\boldsymbol {x}}^{k-1})_i=\sum_{{i_2}, {\cdots}, {i_k}=1}^{n} a_{{i} {i_2} {\cdots} {i_k}} {x_{i_2}}{x_{i_3}}{\cdots}{x_{i_k}}, i \in [n].\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: If there exists $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$ and a nonzero vector $\boldsymbol {x}$ such that \begin{equation}A{\boldsymbol {x}}^{k-1}=\lambda \boldsymbol {x}^{[k-1]},\end{equation} then $\lambda$ is called an eigenvalue of $\mathcal{A}$, and $\boldsymbol {x}$ is an eigenvector of $\mathcal{A}$ corresponding to $\lambda$ \cite{qi2005eigenvalues,lim2005singular , where $\boldsymbol {x ^{[k-1] =(x_1^{k-1 , {\cdots , x_n^{k-1 )^{\mathrm{T }$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The eigenvalues of tensors have attracted extensive attention since they have been proposed \cite{qi2017tensor,cooper2012spectra,sun2016moore,chen2024spectra .}
elements.sent-2411-054: And they have been applied to hypergraph clustering \cite{chang2020hypergraph , image fusion \cite{sun2023nf , crystallography \cite{chen2023c , mechanics \cite{nikabadze2016eigenvalue , and so on.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: A tensor is nonnegative if all its elements are nonnegative.

elements.sent-2411-133: In \cite{zhou2013efficient , researchers proposed an algorithm(ZQW) for computing the spectral radius and corresponding eigenvector of a nonnegative weakly irreducible tensor.}
elements.sent-2411-134: This paper uses the ZQW-algorithm to calculate the subgraph eigenvector centrality of graphs, the algorithm is as follows.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \renewcommand{\algorithmicrequire}{Input:} \renewcommand{\algorithmicensure}{Output:} \caption{The subgraph eigenvector centrality of graphs} \label{A1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require A graph $G$ \Ensure The subgraph eigenvector centrality $\boldsymbol {x}$ \State Form the subgraph tensor $\mathcal{A}_{F}$ by finding all subgraphs in $G$ that are isomorphic to $F$. \State Choose an $n$-dimension vector ${\boldsymbol {x}}^{(0)} > 0$. Let $\mathcal{B}_{F} = \mathcal{A}_{F} + \mathcal{I}$ and $\boldsymbol {y}^{(0)} =\mathcal{B}_{F}{(\boldsymbol {x}^{(0)})^2}$. Set $k=1$. \State Compute \Statex $\boldsymbol {x}^{(k)}= \frac{{\boldsymbol {y}^{(k-1)}}^{[\frac{1}{2}]} } { ||{\boldsymbol {y}^{(k-1)}}^{[\frac{1}{2}]}||} , {\boldsymbol {y}}^{(k)}={\mathcal{B}}_{F}{({\boldsymbol {x}}^{(k)})^2}$ \Statex $\lambda^k =\min_{x_i^{(k)}> 0 } \frac{y_i^{(k)}}{(x_i^{(k)})^2} $ , $\overline{\lambda}_k = \max_{x_i^{(k)}> 0 } \frac{y_i^{(k)}}{(x_i^{(k)})^2}$. \State If $\lambda^k = \overline{\lambda}_k$, the algorithm stops and then $\boldsymbol {x}^{(k)}$ is the subgraph tensor eigenvector centrality; Otherwise, replace $k$ by $k+1$ and go to step 3. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-135: In this section, we calculate the $P_2$-subgraph eigenvector centrality and the ($K_2, K_3$)-subgraph eigenvector centrality in two real-world networks.
elements.sent-2411-136: Moreover we compare and analyze them with eigenvector centrality, betweenness centrality\cite{freeman1991centrality and subgraph centrality\cite{estrada2005subgraph .}}
elements.sent-2411-137: We denote betweenness centrality by $BC$, the expression for $BC$ of vertex $v$ is $C(v)= \sum_{s, v, t \frac{\sigma_{st (v) {\sigma_{st }$, where $s, v, t$ are vertices that are distinct from each other, $\sigma_{st}$ is the total number of shortest paths from vertex $s$ to vertex $t$, and $\sigma_{st (v)$ is the number of shortest paths that pass through vertex $v$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: The subgraph centrality is denoted by $SC$, the expression for $SC$ of vertex $u$ is $C(u)= \left(\exp(A)\right)_{u u = \sum_{k=0 ^{\infty \frac{\left(A^k\right)_{u u {k !}$, where $A$ is the adjacency matrix of graph $G$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: The Sandi-Auths network is a social network consisting of $86$ vertices and $124$ edges.
elements.sent-2411-140: The vertices in the network represent political candidates in San Diego, California, and the edges represent the mutual support relationships between these candidates \cite{walteros2019detecting .}
elements.sent-2411-141: In \cite{bugedo2024family , a centrality measure based on subgraphs counting was proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Specifically, the All-Trees centrality($TS$) has been studied when the subgraphs are trees, and experiment was conducted in Sandi-Auths network.
elements.sent-2411-143: We also use several centrality measures to rank the vertices in Sandi-Auths network, including $EC$, $BC$, $SC$, $P_2C$, $(K_2,K_3)C$ and $TS$.
elements.sent-2411-144: In Table \ref{table4 , we list the top five vertices ranked by the aforementioned six centrality measures.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In Figure \ref{fig11 , we present the heatmap of the Sandi-Auths network under these six centrality measures, and highlight the top five vertices in the graph.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{The top five vertices in Figure 3 under six centrality measures.} \label{table4} \begin{tabular}{lccccccccc} Measures rank1 rank2 rank3 rank4 rank5 $EC$ 33 36 2 44 26 $P_{2}C$ 36 33 30 44 3 $(K_2,K_3)C$ 2 66 18 32 76 $BC$ 33 30 36 58 3 $SC$ 30 36 33 3 2 $TS$ 36 30 40 33 2 \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-006: \subfloat[ $EC$]{ \includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{101.png} } \subfloat[ $P_2C$]{ \includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{102.png} } \subfloat[ $(K_2,K_3)C$]{ \includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{103.png} } \subfloat[$BC$]{ \includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{104.png} } \subfloat[$SC$]{ \includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{105.png} } \subfloat[$TS$]{ \includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{106.png} } \caption{The heatmap of the Sandi-Auths network under six centrality measures.} \label{fig11}
elements.sent-2411-146: From Table \ref{table4 , we can observe that $P_2C$ and $(K_2,K_3)C$ have different rankings from other centrality measures.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Specifically, the ranking under $(K_2,K_3)C$ shows a significant difference when compare to other centrality measures.
elements.sent-2411-148: In the top five rankings of $P_2C$ and $TS$, there are three common vertices.
elements.sent-2411-149: In the top five rankings of $(K_2,K_3)C$ and $TS$, there is only one common vertex.
elements.sent-2411-150: By observing Figure \ref{fig11 , it can be seen that vertices ranking higher in $P_2C$(or $(K_2,K_3)C$) are usually in more subgraph $P_2$(or $K_3$).}
elements.sent-2411-151: However, the ranking of a vertex does not entirely depend on the number of given subgraphs contain the vertex.
elements.sent-2411-152: The centrality measures proposed in this paper are global centrality measures.
elements.sent-2411-153: The Zachary's karate club network, as shown in Figure \ref{fig7 , consists of $34$ vertices and $78$ edges, represent the friendships among club members observed over two years \cite{zachary1977information .}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Due to a disagreement between the club administrator and the instructor, the club split into two smaller groups.
elements.sent-2411-155: We calculate the $P_2C$ and $(K_2,K_3)C$ for the karate club and compare them with $EC$, $BC$ and $SC$.
elements.sent-2411-156: In Figure \ref{fig9 , we plot the scatter plot matrix of $P_2C$, $(K_2,K_3)C$, $EC$, $BC$ and $SC$.}
elements.sent-2411-157: The diagonal elements of the scatter plot matrix are histograms of the score distributions for centrality measures, while the off-diagonal elements represent the correlations between two centrality measures.
elements.sent-2411-158: Compared to other centrality measures, the distribution of centrality scores for vertices under $P_2C$ is more dispersed, and the differences are relatively small.
elements.sent-2411-159: The numerical differences in centrality scores of vertices under $(K_2,K_3)C$ are relatively large.
elements.sent-2411-160: Referring to Table \ref{table3 , we observe that $P_2C$ has a strong correlation with other centrality measures, especially with $EC$, while the correlations of $(K_2,K_3)C$ with other centrality measures are all relatively weak.}
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic71.png} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pic72.png} \end{minipage} \caption{Scatter plot matrix of the five centrality measures of the Zachary's karate club network.} \label{fig9}
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{The top ten vertices in Figure 3 under five centrality measures.} \label{table3} \begin{tabular}{lccccccccc} Measures rank1 rank2 rank3 rank4 rank5 $EC$ 34 1 3 33 2 $P_{2}C$ 34 1 33 3 2 $(K_2,K_3)C$ 1 2 3 4 14 $BC$ 1 34 33 3 32 $SC$ 34 1 33 3 2 Measures rank6 rank7 rank8 rank9 rank10 $EC$ 9 14 4 32 31 $P_{2}C$ 9 14 32 4 31 $(K_2,K_3)C$ 8 9 20 18 22 $BC$ 9 2 14 20 6 $SC$ 4 14 9 32 8 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-161: The eigenvector centrality of graphs considers the centrality of a vertex is influenced by its adjacent vertices, and uses a matrix to represent whether vertices are adjacent, i.e., whether they are on the same edge.
elements.sent-2411-162: As research deepens, we consider that the centrality of a vertex is influenced by the big subgraphs that contain the vertex.
elements.sent-2411-163: Naturally, the relationship between vertices and subgraphs is represented by a tensor.
elements.sent-2411-164: In recent years, tensor has been widely used in various fields to address high-dimensional problems, and it may be an inevitable trend to use tensors to study centrality of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-165: In this paper, for a given structural subgraph $F$ of graph $G$, we consider that the centrality of a vertex $i$ of $G$ is determined by the centrality of other vertices in all subgraphs contain $i$ and isomorphic to $F$.
elements.sent-2411-166: We propose the $F$-subgraph eigenvector centrality and the ($K_2,F$)-subgraph eigenvector centrality of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-167: When we choose $F$ to be $P_1$(or $K_2$), the $F$-subgraph eigenvector centrality is eigenvector centrality of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-168: The necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of both $P_2$-subgraph eigenvector centrality and the ($K_2,F$)-subgraph eigenvector centrality are also provided in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-169: In regular graphs, vertices have the same eigenvector centrality scores.
elements.sent-2411-170: The $(K_2,K_3)$-eigenvector centrality proposed in this paper can distinguish vertices in a given regular graph.
elements.sent-2411-171: We apply the centrality measures proposed in this paper to some real-world networks.
elements.sent-2411-172: The experimental results indicate that vertices with higher centrality scores are generally in a greater number of given subgraphs.
elements.sent-2411-173: The centrality measures proposed in this paper are global centrality measures, the ranking of a vertex does not entirely depend on the number of given subgraphs contain the vertex.. .
